planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings late winter to early spring; Carlos is self-fertile and one of the most reliable muscadines for the Gulf South. It ripens mid-season (August–September in zone 9). Build a strong arbor or high-wire trellis to support vigorous vines.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 6.0–6.5) in full sun; Carlos is a bronze-skinned muscadine developed by North Carolina State University and released in 1970, and remains one of the two or three most widely planted muscadines in the southeastern US because of its exceptional adaptability, consistent yields, and suitability for both fresh-eating and bronze muscadine wine production. The medium-sized, bronze-gold berries ripen uniformly in mid-season and have a mild, sweet musky flavor that is less pronounced than Scuppernong — making Carlos wines and juice more accessible to people new to muscadine flavors. Self-fertile, so it produces well as a single plant, though yields improve with a black muscadine variety nearby. Carlos is resistant to Pierce's disease, downy mildew, and other diseases that devastate European wine grapes in the humid South — no spray program is needed in most home garden settings. Prune in late winter, leaving only short spurs on permanent cordons. Harvest when berries slip from the cluster at a gentle tug.
sources: North Carolina State University — Carlos muscadine release notes 1970
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Muscadine Varieties (B 1145)
LSU AgCenter — Muscadine Production in Louisiana
Ison's Nursery and Vineyard muscadine variety notes

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in late winter to early spring; Noble is self-fertile and mid-to-late season (September in zone 9). One of the best black muscadines for wine, juice, and jelly production due to its very high anthocyanin and resveratrol content.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; Noble is a black-skinned muscadine released by North Carolina State University in 1971 and named for James Noble, an early muscadine researcher. It is the standard against which black muscadine wine varieties are measured: the deep purple-black berries are extraordinarily high in anthocyanins, resveratrol, and other polyphenols that give muscadine wine and juice its distinctive deep color and purported health benefits. Flavor is rich, musky, and complex — more intensely flavored than bronze varieties — producing deep-colored wine, juice, and jelly with earthy, berry notes. Fresh-eating quality is good but not as mild as bronze types; the thick skin and seeds are more pronounced. Noble is self-fertile and produces consistently high yields even as a single vine. Its exceptional disease resistance and adaptability make it one of the best black muscadines for the humid Gulf South. Harvest when berries are fully black and yield easily to gentle pressure.
sources: North Carolina State University — Noble muscadine release notes 1971
North Carolina Muscadine Grape Association variety guide
LSU AgCenter — Red and Purple Muscadine Wine Production
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Muscadine Grape Production

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in late winter to early spring; Cowart is self-fertile and one of the earliest black muscadines to ripen (August in zone 9), making it the first black-skinned muscadine in the season. Excellent paired with Carlos or Fry for a mixed muscadine planting.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Cowart is an early-ripening black muscadine developed by the Georgia Experiment Station (now UGA) and released in 1974. It is widely valued as a dual-purpose black muscadine: the large, jet-black berries (larger than Noble) are excellent for fresh eating with a rich, sweet, full-flavored muscadine taste, and also produce outstanding dark wine, juice, and jam. As one of the earliest-ripening black muscadines, Cowart extends the beginning of the black muscadine season and is frequently paired with Noble and other later-ripening varieties to spread harvest over a longer window. The thick skin makes berries more durable and longer-lasting after harvest than most muscadines. Cowart is self-fertile and requires no pollinizer, though yields increase with nearby compatible varieties. Disease resistance is excellent across the humid Gulf South. Harvest in late August when berries are fully black and slip freely from the stem with gentle pressure.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Muscadine Grape Varieties for Georgia
Georgia Experiment Station — Cowart muscadine release notes 1974
LSU AgCenter Publication — Muscadines for Louisiana Home Gardens
Mississippi State University Extension — Muscadine Grape Production

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in late winter to early spring; IMPORTANT: Fry is NOT self-fertile and requires a self-fertile pollinizer (Carlos, Noble, Cowart) within 50 feet for fruit set. Without cross-pollination, Fry produces little to no fruit. Mid-season ripening (August–September in zone 9).

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun alongside a self-fertile pollinizer variety; Fry is a bronze muscadine developed by the Georgia Experiment Station and released in 1972, consistently regarded as the best fresh-eating muscadine available — the benchmark for table-quality muscadine grapes. The berries are extraordinarily large (up to 1.5 inches in diameter, one of the largest of any muscadine), bronze-gold at maturity with a beautiful appearance, excellent mild-sweet flavor, and thinner skin than most muscadines, making fresh eating more pleasant. Fry's principal limitation is its lack of self-fertility: it will produce essentially no fruit without a compatible self-fertile pollinizer planted within range. This has made it less popular commercially where single-variety plantings are common, but in home gardens where a pollinator is easily added, Fry is unmatched for fresh table quality. The vines are vigorous, productive once pollinated, and disease-resistant. Harvest when berries are fully bronze-gold and yield to gentle pressure.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Fresh Market Muscadine Varieties
Georgia Experiment Station — Fry muscadine release notes 1972
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Muscadine Grape Production in Texas
Ison's Nursery muscadine variety notes

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in late winter to early spring; Doreen is self-fertile and a very late-season muscadine ripening in September–October in zone 9, the last of the major commercial varieties to ripen. This late ripening extends the muscadine harvest season and makes Doreen valuable for bronze wine blending.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; Doreen is a bronze muscadine released by Mississippi State University in 1979 and named for a long-serving clerical employee at the university's experiment station. It is the most widely used late-season bronze muscadine, valued by commercial growers and home winemakers for extending the harvest season past Carlos and Scuppernong into late September and October. Yields are among the highest of any self-fertile bronze variety, making Doreen important for commercial production where volume matters. Berries are medium-sized, bronze-tan at maturity with a pleasant, mild muscadine flavor and good sugar content that makes excellent late-season wine. The plants are very vigorous and productive with strong disease resistance. Doreen is often planted in combination with Carlos to extend the bronze muscadine season from mid-August through October and to provide a range of flavors for blending. Prune heavily each winter; leave only short spurs on permanent cordon arms.
sources: Mississippi State University Extension — Muscadine Cultivar Evaluation
Mississippi Agricultural and Forestry Experiment Station — Doreen release notes 1979
LSU AgCenter — Muscadine Wine Production in Louisiana
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Muscadine Varieties
